description: The Cigna Group is a U.S.-based global health company operating through Cigna Healthcare and Evernorth Health Services, combining insurance, pharmacy benefit management, care coordination, and related health services.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Cigna is one of the major managed-care companies in the United States, with a model that pairs traditional health benefits with a large health-services platform. Its core public-facing businesses are Cigna Healthcare, the insurance and benefits arm, and Evernorth Health Services, the services arm that includes pharmacy, care management, specialty pharmacy, and related capabilities.

The company describes itself as serving more than 185 million customer relationships across more than 30 markets and jurisdictions. That scale gives Cigna substantial leverage in benefit design, pharmacy contracting, claims administration, and provider access, which is either efficient coordination or an impressive amount of paperwork wearing a wellness badge, depending on where one sits in the system.

overview.title: Product And Platform Map
paragraphs: Cigna Healthcare covers employer, individual, government, international, dental, behavioral, and supplemental health benefit offerings. These products are the most visible consumer layer of the business, where members encounter plan networks, claims rules, digital portals, care navigation, and customer service.

Evernorth is the deeper infrastructure layer. It houses health-services capabilities such as pharmacy benefit management, Express Scripts, specialty pharmacy, medical benefit management, and care coordination services that can be sold to Cigna plans and third-party clients. For Free The World purposes, Evernorth is especially relevant because it sits between patients, employers, pharmacies, drug manufacturers, and providers.

overview.title: Registry Relevance
paragraphs: Cigna belongs in the registry as a high-impact health-care intermediary rather than a pure technology company. Its influence comes from administrative systems, formularies, claims processing, network design, prior authorization workflows, and vertically integrated health-service assets.

Open and user-sovereign alternatives will likely map less to a single product replacement and more to categories such as transparent benefits administration, patient-controlled health records, direct care networks, open drug-price data, independent care navigation, and decentralized consent or claims infrastructure.
